content.text: Thinking in React: Decomposing a UI into a Component Tree
The hardest skill to develop when learning React is not writing JSX or wiring up state — it is knowing
how to slice a design into components in the first place

. The React team published a five-step process called "Thinking in React" that turns any UI mockup into a maintainable component tree. This document focuses on the first two steps: breaking the UI into a hierarchy and building a static version.

attrs.alt: A product-table mockup on the left with colour-coded component boundaries, and the resulting five-node component hierarchy on the right

content.text: The Five-Step Process
The complete Thinking in React process is:
Break the UI into a component hierarchy
— draw boxes around every distinct piece.
Build a static version in React
— render the hierarchy with hard-coded data, no state yet.
Find the minimal and complete representation of state
— identify what must actually change over time.
Identify where state should live
— find the common ancestor of every component that reads or writes each piece of state.
Add inverse data flow
— pass callbacks from parents to children so children can update state.
Steps 3–5 involve state and will be covered in later topics. Steps 1–2 are about structure and are the focus here.
Step 1: Break the UI into a Component Hierarchy
Start with the design mockup (a Figma file, a sketch, a screenshot). Draw a coloured border around every distinct visual region you can identify. Each bordered region becomes one component.
The standard heuristic for deciding where to draw a border is the
single-responsibility principle
: a component should do exactly one thing. If it ends up doing more than one thing, break it into sub-components. If it is too small to warrant its own file, merge it upward.
For the product table example above, five components emerge naturally:
FilterableProductTable
— the root; owns the combination of search and list.
SearchBar
— the text input and checkbox.
ProductTable
— the table and its rows.
ProductCategoryRow
— a coloured heading row separating categories.
ProductRow
— a single product line (name, price, stock status).
Nesting in the design maps directly to nesting in the tree:
ProductRow
sits inside
ProductTable
, which sits inside
FilterableProductTable
. Sibling regions become sibling components.
Decomposition Heuristics
Beyond single-responsibility, these practical tests help draw boundaries:
Signal
What it means
Region appears more than once
Strong candidate for a reusable component.
Backed by a data model field
If a JSON object or array maps to a region, that region is a component.
Visual separation in the design
Distinct backgrounds, borders, or spacing usually signal a component boundary.
Independent interactivity
A region that can expand, collapse, filter, or animate on its own is its own component.
Designer named it
Design systems and Figma layers often name components — use those names.
Mapping the Hierarchy to Code Files
Once you have the hierarchy, the file structure follows directly. One component per file is the standard convention in almost all React projects:

src/
  components/
    FilterableProductTable.jsx   ← root
    SearchBar.jsx
    ProductTable.jsx
    ProductCategoryRow.jsx
    ProductRow.jsx
Smaller leaf components (like
ProductCategoryRow
or
ProductRow
) can co-locate in the same file as their parent until they need independent reuse or become large enough to warrant their own file.
Step 2: Build a Static Version
With the hierarchy in hand, build the entire UI as
static components
— components that accept data via props and render it, with no
useState
or event handlers yet. This is the fastest way to verify that your hierarchy is right.

No state. No event handlers. Every component is a pure function of its props. The hierarchy is visible in the JSX nesting. This version should render correctly in the browser before you add interactivity.
Why Static First?
Building static and interactive at the same time makes debugging harder because you cannot tell whether a bug is in the structure or the state logic. The static phase answers one question cleanly:
is my component hierarchy correct?
If the static version looks right, the hierarchy is right, and you can add interactivity on a solid foundation.
In static phase, data flows top-down through props. This is the only flow direction you need before state exists. It trains the habit of thinking in one-way data flow from the start.
Recognising a Good Decomposition
After building the static version, check the decomposition against these properties:
Each component has a name you can explain in one phrase
— if you struggle to name it, it may be doing too many things.
No two components need the same piece of data independently
— if they do, their common ancestor should hold that data and pass it as props.
Leaf components are pure and small
— they should be easy to test in isolation.
The tree depth is proportional to the design's actual nesting
— if the tree is unexpectedly deep, you may have over-extracted.
Common Decomposition Mistakes
One giant component
— a 300-line component trying to render a whole page. Should be split along visual regions.
Extracting single elements
— making a
Title
component that renders exactly one
with no variation. Add abstraction only when it will be reused or gains behaviour.
Mixing layout and logic in one component
— the component both fetches data and renders UI. Fetching and rendering should be separate concerns (covered in data-fetching topics).
Decomposing by file rather than by concern
— splitting a component only because the file got long, without a conceptual boundary, creates components that must talk to each other in complicated ways.
Iterating on the Hierarchy

The first decomposition is rarely final. As you implement the static version, you will notice that two components share data awkwardly, or that a component is doing two things. Refactoring the hierarchy early — before state is involved — is cheap. Reorganising after you have wired up state, callbacks, and Context is expensive.
Concrete triggers for iteration:
A prop is passed through a component that never reads it (prop drilling). Consider splitting the wrapper into two siblings instead.
A component's JSX has two clearly separate visual sections. Extract each into its own component.
The same JSX block appears in two places. Make it one component.
Data Model → Component Shape
Well-designed data models and well-designed component trees tend to mirror each other. If your API returns:
The natural component tree is
CategoryList → Category → ProductRow
— each JSON shape maps to one component. When the tree and the data model align, props flow naturally and there is little need for data transformation in intermediate components.
A Real-World Worked Example
Suppose you are building a simple blog homepage. The mockup has a hero, a list of posts, and a sidebar with recent tags. Here is one way the decomposition could look:

BlogHomePage
├── Hero            (headline + call-to-action)
├── TwoColumnLayout
│   ├── PostList
│   │   └── PostCard (×N)   (title, date, excerpt)
│   └── Sidebar
│       ├── TagCloud
│       │   └── Tag (×N)
│       └── NewsletterSignup
The static version in code:
Each component has a single responsibility. The tree depth (4 levels) matches the visual nesting in the design. The static version can be rendered and reviewed before any state is introduced.
Applying the Process to a Dashboard
Another common UI pattern is an analytics dashboard. Suppose you have a mockup with a top nav, a row of summary cards, a chart area, and a recent-activity feed. Applying the decomposition process:

DashboardPage
├── TopNav
│   ├── Logo
│   └── UserMenu
├── MetricCardRow
│   └── MetricCard (×4)    (label, value, trend indicator)
├── ChartSection
│   ├── RevenueChart
│   └── ChartLegend
└── ActivityFeed
    └── ActivityItem (×N)  (icon, description, timestamp)
Notice how each region maps to a single concern:
MetricCard
knows nothing about the chart;
ActivityFeed
is completely independent. If the design changes MetricCard's colour scheme, only that file changes. This is the payoff of a clean decomposition.
Container vs Presentational Components
An informal but useful distinction divides components into two roles:
Role
Responsibility
Example
Presentational
Renders UI given props; no side effects
ProductRow
Tag
Button
Container
Owns state, fetches data, coordinates children
FilterableProductTable
BlogHomePage
This is not a rigid rule — with hooks, the boundary has blurred. But it is a helpful
thinking tool

during decomposition: identify which components will own behaviour (containers) and which will purely render (presentational). In the static phase, everything is presentational; containers get their behaviour added later.
Building Up vs Drilling Down
When building the static version, you can work in two directions:
Top-down
— start with the root and drill inward. Good for simple UIs where the structure is clear up front. You see the full shape immediately.
Bottom-up
— build leaf components first (
ProductRow
Tag
), then assemble them into larger containers. Preferred for larger UIs — each small piece is tested in isolation before being composed.
Both produce the same result. Bottom-up is generally safer for non-trivial UIs because you confirm that small pieces work before connecting them.
When the Hierarchy Changes Over Time
Designs evolve. A component you thought was a single piece becomes two. A feature that was a separate page moves into a sidebar. The hierarchy must evolve with the design.

React component trees are easy to refactor because each component is self-contained. To split one component into two, extract the JSX, create a new file, and import it. To merge two, move the JSX inline. The surrounding code does not change as long as the props contract stays the same.

The cost of refactoring grows sharply once state and Context are involved. That is why the Thinking in React process explicitly separates decomposition (step 1) from state design (steps 3–4): get the structure right before adding the parts that are hard to move.
Props in the Static Version

During the static phase, props carry all the data. Understanding which props each component needs is the most concrete output of the decomposition exercise. A useful technique: write the props for each component as a type annotation or comment before writing the JSX.

If you find that a component deep in the tree needs a piece of data that has to pass through several intermediate components that do not use it, that is a sign the hierarchy may be wrong, or that you will eventually need React Context.
From Static to Interactive: What Changes

Once the static version renders correctly, adding interactivity follows a predictable pattern. You replace hard-coded prop values with state variables, and you pass callbacks down to components that trigger changes. The component
structure
does not change — only the data flowing through it does.
The hierarchy is identical.
SearchBar
and
ProductTable
remain sibling children of
FilterableProductTable
. The root now holds state and derives the filtered list; children receive what they need via props.
Checking Your Decomposition Against the Design System

Most teams work with a design system (Material UI, Tailwind UI, shadcn/ui, etc.). Your component hierarchy should reuse design-system primitives at the leaf level rather than reimplementing them. This affects decomposition: leaf nodes become the design system's
Button
Input
, and
Card
components rather than custom HTML.
The tree structure is the same. Only the leaf elements change. Identifying which design-system components map to which visual regions is itself part of the decomposition exercise.
Summary
Draw borders around distinct visual regions; each becomes one component.
Use single-responsibility as the primary guide: one component, one job.
Nesting in the design maps directly to parent–child in the component tree.
Build a static version first — props only, no state — to confirm the hierarchy is correct.
Well-structured data models and component trees mirror each other.
Iterate early: restructuring before state is added is cheap; after is costly.
